summary: Gruber describes his search for a lightweight alternative to Growl for displaying script status messages on Mac OS X. After a clean Leopard installation, he found he didn't miss Growl enough to reinstall it immediately, mainly because it consumed 30 MB of memory just for occasional script notifications. He discovered that FastScripts by Daniel Jalkut offers a simple 'display message' command that provides Growl-like floating HUD notifications without the overhead. He provides AppleScript examples and suggests minor improvements like support for a secondary message string and custom icons.
key_points: Growl's system-wide self-dismissing notifications are useful but overkill for occasional script messages, consuming at least 30 MB of memory
A clean OS install revealed which third-party utilities were truly missed versus habitual — Growl wasn't high on the list
FastScripts offers a lightweight HUD-style 'display message' command with configurable position and auto-dismiss timing
The feature could be improved with support for a secondary smaller-font message and custom icons
money_quotes: When I upgraded to Leopard full-time a few months ago, I started with a clean installation and only added back third-party UI utilities one-by-one, when I actually missed them.
Using Growl just for the occasional message from a script seemed like overkill; on my machine Growl typically consumes at least 30 MB of real memory.
It ended up I didn't miss Growl much, and didn't get around to re-installing it until last week.
FastScripts's message window is a simple, dark, slightly tranclucent HUD-style floating window that accepts a single string as a parameter.
But it's a nice lightweight alternative to Growl as-is.
key_insight: A clean OS install is a useful forcing function for discovering which utilities you actually need versus which you run out of habit, and lightweight alternatives often serve better than feature-heavy tools.
